segments.title: How large was William's invasion fleet?
segments.summary: Army size, ship crews, repeated crossings, and fragmentary records narrow extravagant medieval claims toward an invasion fleet of roughly 700 to 800 vessels.
segments.body: The chroniclers' figure of 3,000 ships sits uneasily beside an army usually estimated near 7,000 men. Dr. Clarke explains how later supply voyages, reused vessels, dispersed arrivals, and incomplete administrative records could produce conflicting totals without requiring every ship to cross in one wave.

evidence.note: The transcript compares chronicler totals with army size, crewing requirements, later crossings, and the surviving documentary record.

segments.title: Norman ports and vassals assemble the fleet
segments.summary: William drew ships from powerful vassals and port towns, while Matilda of Flanders supplied the specially built flagship Mora.
segments.body: Ship lists connect the largest contributions to nobles controlling useful ports, making fleet assembly an extension of feudal power and obligation. The rushed construction also meant many vessels were campaign assets rather than durable ships, in contrast to the better-built Mora.

evidence.note: The transcript traces ship contributions to vassals and ports, then contrasts hurried vessels with Matilda's flagship.

segments.title: Why the surviving fleet totals disagree
segments.summary: Figures such as 696 and 777 may describe different moments—ships assembled, ships departing, or vessels surviving storms—rather than mutually exclusive fleet totals.
segments.body: No complete pay roll or operational ship register survives to settle the number. The remaining lists provide valuable snapshots, but historians must distinguish the force mobilized from the force that actually sailed together on 27–28 September 1066.

evidence.note: The transcript compares the 696 and 777 totals and emphasizes the absence of a complete administrative record.

segments.title: Clinker construction made rapid mobilization possible
segments.summary: Shell-first clinker construction let expert overseers turn abundant axe-skilled labor into usable invasion vessels quickly.
segments.body: Builders worked outward from the keel, overlapping shaped planks so the shell supplied the hull's primary strength before frames and thwarts reinforced it. The method concentrated demanding judgment in a smaller number of masters while allowing competent carpenters and woodcutters to carry out much of the labor.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the shell-first sequence, structural role of the planking, and labor requirements.

segments.title: Merchant cogs carried horses and logistics
segments.summary: The Bayeux Tapestry's longships are only part of the story: broader merchant hulls were essential for transporting horses and supplies.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that early clinker-built cogs, probably hired from commercial service, offered the volume and endurance needed for Norman cavalry and its support train. Purpose-built warships could be disposable campaign assets, while merchants expected their cargo vessels to remain economically useful afterward.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts longships with merchant cogs and explains why horses required roomier transport hulls.

segments.title: Nail-and-rove fastenings strengthened clinker hulls
segments.summary: Iron nails clenched into diamond-shaped roves produced strong pseudo-riveted plank joints with period-appropriate speed and reliability.
segments.body: Northern French shipbuilders had refined a fastening technique well suited to William's urgent program. Its value lies in comparison with other eleventh-century methods: it delivered a robust wooden shell quickly, not a modern standard of hull strength.

evidence.note: The transcript describes nails driven through overlapping planks and clenched into roves.

segments.title: An amphibious transport fleet, not a battle fleet
segments.summary: Longships, cogs, and varied Norse-derived designs formed a mixed lift force whose main purpose was delivering troops, horses, and stores.
segments.body: The vessels reflected different regional shipbuilding traditions among William's lords rather than one uniform Norman type. Their tactical purpose was movement and landing; sea fighting in this tradition usually meant closing and boarding rather than operating as a later gun-armed battle fleet.

evidence.note: The transcript characterizes the fleet's mixed Norse heritage and its troop-transport purpose.

segments.title: Early cogs and the invasion's second wave
segments.summary: Calling the transports early cogs clarifies their form, while a 7,000-man first wave followed by reinforcements helps reconcile competing army estimates.
segments.body: The vessels occupy a transitional category between familiar longships and later mature cogs, but the merchant label better conveys their cargo role. Crewmen may also have joined the army after landing, further complicating attempts to separate embarked soldiers, sailors, and follow-on forces.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses the transitional cog label, staged reinforcement, and crews becoming soldiers.

segments.title: Feudal organization spread cost and administration
segments.summary: William mobilized a decentralized coalition whose nobles financed, crewed, supplied, and administered their own contingents in exchange for English rewards.
segments.body: The promise of land and wealth aligned powerful Norman interests behind the invasion while directing an aggressive elite outward. Different contributors produced different ships and captains; Matilda's priority was narrower, providing William with the well-built Mora and a trusted navigator while she governed Normandy.

evidence.note: The transcript explains feudal cost-sharing, contingent administration, conquest incentives, and Matilda's provision of the Mora.

segments.title: The Mora led a scattered fleet across the Channel
segments.summary: Despite storm dispersal and unequal sailing performance, the Mora's stern lantern helped guide the crossing and the invasion force reached England.
segments.body: Some losses may represent ships separated rather than sunk, another reason departure and arrival totals differ. The flagship was sufficiently large and fast to outrun its companions, leaving William temporarily alone offshore before the rest of the force appeared.

evidence.note: The transcript describes storm dispersal, the Mora's lantern, its speed, and the fleet's successful arrival.

segments.title: Why William's invasion fleet disappeared
segments.summary: After landing, ships became fortification timber, returned to trade and fishing, supported further operations, decayed, or survived briefly as prestige vessels.
segments.body: There was no permanent unified fleet to vanish: William had assembled a temporary mixture of borrowed, hired, and newly built craft. Continued pacification required shipping, but scarce timber and fittings also made damaged hulls valuable material, while vessels such as the Mora retained a royal role until they wore out.

evidence.note: The transcript follows vessels into construction timber, trade, fishing, logistics, reuse, decay, and royal service.

segments.title: The ship list is a later but essential source
segments.summary: The surviving Battle manuscript ship list was compiled roughly 70 to 100 years after 1066, making it indispensable but not contemporary bureaucracy.
segments.body: The manuscript preserves information drawn from records and memories available to its compilers, yet its date limits the precision historians can claim. It is best treated as a starting point to compare with other evidence rather than a complete operational return.

evidence.note: The transcript dates the Battle manuscript to about 1130–1160 and explains its evidentiary value and limitations.
